lesson.title: CAPS.Mathematics.Gr12.P1.FIN: Future value and present value annuities: F = x[(1+i)^n −...
lesson.explanation: Compound growth: A = P(1+i)^n. Compound decay: A = P(1-i)^n. Nominal rate is the stated annual rate; effective rate accounts for compounding: 1 + i_eff = (1 + i_nom/m)^m where m is compounding periods per year. Depreciation: straight-line (fixed amount per year) vs reducing balance (fixed percentage of current value). Always convert rates to match the compounding period.

examples.problem: You take out a loan of R200 000 at 12% p.a. compounded monthly. Calculate the monthly repayment over 5 years.

steps.instruction: Step 1: i = 0.12/12 = 0.01 per month, n = 5 × 12 = 60 months

examples.problem: You save R500 per month at 9% p.a. compounded monthly. How much do you have after 3 years?
You save R500 per month at 9% p.a. compounded monthly. How much do you have after 3 years?

practice_problems.question: Thandi invests R50 000 in a savings account at 8% p.a. compounded quarterly. How much will she have after 5 years?
hints: Use the compound interest formula A = P(1 + i)ⁿ.
Compounded quarterly means i = 0.08/4 = 0.02 per quarter, and n = 5 × 4 = 20 quarters.

practice_problems.question: A bank offers a nominal interest rate of 9.5% p.a. compounded monthly. Calculate the effective annual interest rate.
hints: Use the effective rate formula: 1 + i_eff = (1 + i_nom/m)^m.

practice_problems.question: Sipho wants to save R365 000 for a deposit on a house in 8 years. He opens a sinking fund that earns 10% p.a. compounded monthly. What must his monthly payment be?
hints: This is a future value annuity (sinking fund). Use F = x[(1+i)ⁿ − 1]/i.

practice_problems.question: Naledi takes out a home loan of R800 000 at 11.5% p.a. compounded monthly, to be repaid over 20 years. Calculate her monthly repayment.
hints: This is a present value annuity (loan repayment). Use P = x[1 − (1+i)^(−n)]/i.

practice_problems.question: A loan of R500 000 is taken at 12% p.a. compounded monthly, to be repaid in equal monthly instalments over 20 years. Calculate the outstanding balance on the loan after 8 years of payments.
hints: First find the monthly payment using the PV annuity formula with n = 240.
The balance outstanding after k payments equals the PV of the remaining (n − k) payments. Use the same payment x with n = 240 − 96 = 144.

practice_problems.question: Lerato considers two investment options. Option A: R1 000 per month into an account earning 9% p.a. compounded monthly for 10 years. Option B: A lump sum of R100 000 invested at 10% p.a. compounded annually for 10 years. Which option yields more, and by how much?
practice_problems.answer: Option B yields more, by R65 859.98. (Option A = R193 514.27; Option B = R259 374.25)
hints: For Option A, use the FV annuity formula with i = 0.09/12 = 0.0075 and n = 120.
For Option B, use A = P(1+i)ⁿ with P = 100 000, i = 0.10, n = 10.
Compare the two final amounts to determine the difference.

practice_problems.question: A car costs R480 000. Kagiso pays a 10% deposit and agrees to a balloon payment of 20% of the original price at the end of the loan. The remaining amount is financed at 9.5% p.a. compounded monthly over 5 years. Calculate the monthly instalment and the total amount Kagiso pays for the car (deposit + instalments + balloon).
practice_problems.answer: Monthly instalment = R7 803.11; Total paid = R612 186.60
hints: The loan amount is R480 000 − R48 000 = R432 000, but you must subtract the present value of the balloon payment.
PV of balloon = 96 000 × (1.007917)^(−60). The annuity portion is R432 000 − PV(balloon). Then use the PV annuity formula to find x.
Are you saving (future value) or paying off a loan (present value)?
For loans, use P = x[1 − (1+i)⁻ⁿ]/i. Convert annual rate to monthly.
i = annual rate ÷ 12, n = years × 12. Substitute into the PV formula and solve for x.

key_vocabulary.definition: A series of regular, equal payments made at fixed intervals
key_vocabulary.example_usage: A home loan with monthly payments is an annuity.

key_vocabulary.definition: Money saved regularly to replace an asset or pay a future expense
key_vocabulary.example_usage: The company set up a sinking fund for equipment replacement.

key_vocabulary.definition: The current worth of a future sum or series of payments
key_vocabulary.example_usage: The present value of the loan determines your monthly payment.

key_vocabulary.definition: The annual interest rate before adjusting for compounding frequency
key_vocabulary.example_usage: A nominal rate of 12% p.a. compounded monthly means 1% per month.

key_vocabulary.definition: The actual annual rate after accounting for compounding
key_vocabulary.example_usage: A nominal 12% monthly gives an effective rate of 12.68%.

misconceptions.misconception: Students use the annual interest rate directly instead of converting to monthly.
misconceptions.correction: If payments are monthly, divide the annual rate by 12: i = r/12.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: The payment period must match the compounding period in the formula.

assessment.title: Assessment: Future value and present value annuities: F = x[(1+i)^n − 1]/i, P = x[1 − (1+i)^−n]/i. Sinking funds, loan repayments, outstanding balance. Effective vs nominal rates. Deferred payments. Pyramidal schemes

items.question: Explain the difference between a future value annuity and a present value annuity. Give an example of each.
items.correct_answer: FV: saving towards a goal (e.g., retirement fund). PV: paying off a loan (e.g., home bond).

flashcards.front: What is the present value annuity formula?
flashcards.back: P = x[1 − (1+i)⁻ⁿ]/i, where x = payment, i = rate per period, n = number of periods
